ocar.opportunity: The mushroom body (MB) is the primary learning and memory center in insects, but its complete synaptic wiring — essential for understanding how memories are formed and retrieved — had not been determined in any species.
ocar.challenge: Reconstructing the complete mushroom body connectome requires tracing thousands of Kenyon cells and their inputs from olfactory projection neurons and modulatory neurons at EM resolution, demanding both imaging and reconstruction resources at the time unprecedented for an insect brain structure.
ocar.action: The authors reconstructed the complete connectome of the Drosophila larval mushroom body from serial-section EM, tracing all Kenyon cells, olfactory PNs, dopaminergic and serotonergic modulatory neurons, and output neurons with their full synaptic connectivity.
ocar.resolution: The complete MB connectome revealed sparse, random-like odor coding by Kenyon cells, systematic compartmentalization of dopaminergic modulation aligned with valence, and the organizational logic by which output neurons read out the memory trace. Key circuit motifs provided direct evidence for specific memory encoding and retrieval models.
ocar.future_work: Future work should map the adult Drosophila MB connectome to determine how wiring changes during development, and functionally validate the circuit predictions from the larval connectome using optogenetics and calcium imaging.

summaries.beginner: Fruit fly larvae can learn to avoid bad smells. The part of their brain responsible for this — the mushroom body — was completely mapped using an electron microscope to show every single connection. The map revealed how memories might be stored and then retrieved.
summaries.intermediate: The complete serial-section EM connectome of the Drosophila larval mushroom body revealed sparse Kenyon cell odor coding, valence-compartmentalized dopaminergic input, and structured output neuron readout. This circuit architecture directly supports models of associative memory in which dopamine shifts Kenyon cell synaptic weights to store learned odor valences.
summaries.advanced: This complete larval MB connectome from serial-section EM provides synaptic-resolution evidence for the circuit logic of insect associative learning. Key findings include that KC-to-output neuron connectivity is structured rather than fully random, that dopaminergic neurons compartmentalize the MB lobes in a valence-specific manner, and that APL provides global inhibition implementing sparse coding. Limitations include the larval-stage restriction (adult MB is larger and more complex), the static nature of the connectome (cannot capture plasticity), and assumptions that synapse number correlates with functional strength.

discussion_prompts: What circuit features of the mushroom body connectome support or challenge the Hebbian-like model of associative memory storage, and what remains to be tested functionally?
How does the organization of dopaminergic inputs revealed by the connectome map onto the valence-specific modulation known from behavioral and functional studies?
What are the key differences expected between the larval and adult Drosophila mushroom body connectomes, and how would those differences change our understanding of memory circuits?
